name: Didyma Oracle
description: The Didyma Oracle, located near Miletus on the coast of Anatolia, was one of the most important oracular sanctuaries in the ancient Greek world, dedicated to Apollo Didymaeus. Unlike Delphi, Didyma was not a city-state but rather a pan-Ionian sanctuary administered by the city of Miletus. The oracle's significance stemmed from its reputation for delivering accurate and insightful prophecies, attracting pilgrims and suppliants from across the Greek world seeking guidance on matters of state, personal affairs, and religious practices. The prophecies were initially delivered through a spring and the murmuring of a laurel tree, interpreted by the priests, but later a female prophetess delivered oracles in a state of divine possession. The sanctuary was particularly renowned for its vast temple, the Didymaion, which was one of the largest and most impressive structures in the Hellenistic world, reflecting the wealth and influence associated with the oracle. Although destroyed and rebuilt multiple times throughout its history, Didyma remained a vital center of religious and political influence for centuries, underscoring Apollo's power and the enduring belief in divine guidance in the ancient world. Alexander the Great is even said to have confirmed its importance. The site's grandeur, coupled with its oracular pronouncements, solidified its place in Greek mythology and history, linking it to significant events and figures.
subtitle: Apollo's Prophetic Sanctuary near Miletus

keyMyths.title: Foundation Myth
keyMyths.description: Legend states that Apollo seduced a local girl, resulting in the birth of Branchus, who was granted prophetic powers by his father, thus establishing the oracle. This established a lineage of prophetic individuals connected to Apollo's divine favor.

keyMyths.title: Alexander's Visit
keyMyths.description: After the siege of Miletus, Alexander the Great is said to have visited Didyma and received confirmation of his divine lineage and future successes. This event further enhanced the oracle's prestige and associated it with the era's most powerful figure.

sources.author: Herodotus
sources.description: Provides early accounts of the oracle's influence and interactions with various Greek city-states.
sources.text: Provides early accounts of the oracle's influence and interactions with various Greek city-states.

sources.author: Pausanias
sources.description: Offers a detailed description of the Didymaion and its religious practices during the Roman period.
